Taiwan Semiconductor.role: Dominant pure-play foundry manufacturing the world's most advanced chips for AI accelerators, mobile, and HPC; near-monopoly at the leading edge anchors durable demand, though geopolitical concentration and capex cyclicality cap permanence and elasticity.

ASML Holding.role: Sole supplier of EUV lithography systems essential to advanced-node chip manufacturing; near-absolute moat anchors quality and dominance, but lumpy WFE capex cycles and customer concentration leave it cyclically exposed despite its monopoly position.

Lam Research.role: Lam dominates etch and deposition equipment essential for advanced logic and especially memory (HBM/DRAM/NAND) production; it benefits from AI-driven capex but earnings remain tied to cyclical WFE spending cycles.

KLA Corporation.role: KLA dominates semiconductor process control and inspection/metrology equipment with ~50%+ share, a wide moat tied to rising chip complexity and AI-driven leading-edge investment, but as a WFE supplier its revenue remains tied to cyclical fab capex.

Synopsys.role: Synopsys is a duopoly leader in EDA software and IP critical to all advanced chip design; recurring, high-switching-cost revenue makes demand durable across cycles, with AI-driven design complexity a long-term tailwind.

Cadence Design Systems.role: Cadence provides essential EDA software and IP for designing advanced chips, a recurring, near-duopoly franchise with AMD/Nvidia-grade customers, placing it in durable AI-infra with high TAM credibility and low terminal risk.

Monolithic Power Systems.role: Fabless analog/power-management IC maker supplying voltage regulation for AI server GPUs and data-center boards; sits in AI-infra with solid moat but cyclical exposure to data-center capex and a rich valuation that tempers earnings durability.

Analog Devices.role: ADI is a leading high-performance analog and mixed-signal chipmaker serving industrial, automotive, comms, and data-center markets; durable franchise with broad design wins but cyclical exposure tempers its AI-infra positioning.

Lattice Semiconductor.role: Leading vendor of small low-power FPGAs for edge, industrial, and communications systems; recovering from an inventory-correction cycle with modest single-digit revenue growth, giving it a niche moat but cyclical, capex-sensitive demand that tempers its AI-infra positioning.

Credo Technology.role: Credo supplies high-speed connectivity solutions (active electrical cables, SerDes, retimers) for AI data centers; strong hyperscaler-driven growth but mid-tier moat and high customer concentration leave it cyclically exposed to AI capex swings.

MACOM Technology.role: MACOM supplies RF, microwave, and high-speed optical/analog semiconductors for data-center, telecom, and defense markets; modest recent growth and diversified non-pure-play AI exposure place it as a mid-quality cyclical AI-infra name with meaningful but not dominant share.

Allegro MicroSystems.role: Allegro makes magnetic sensors and power management ICs heavily exposed to automotive (EV/ADAS) and industrial markets; it is a cyclical mid-cap semiconductor with solid niche position in Hall-effect sensing but limited direct AI-data-center leverage, hence moderate moat and cyclical earnings.

Teradyne.role: Teradyne is a leading supplier of automated test equipment for semiconductors, benefiting from AI/HPC chip complexity driving test intensity, but its revenue is tied to cyclical semiconductor capex and equipment order swings.

Onto Innovation.role: Onto supplies metrology, inspection and lithography systems for semiconductor and advanced packaging fabs, benefiting from AI-driven packaging complexity but exposed to cyclical fab capex; modest TTM growth tempers the near-term outlook.

Camtek.role: Camtek supplies metrology and inspection equipment critical to advanced packaging (HBM, 2.5D/3D) for AI chips; strong secular tailwind but exposure to cyclical semicap capex limits durability and elasticity.

FormFactor.role: FormFactor makes probe cards and test systems used in semiconductor wafer testing; it benefits from AI/HBM/advanced-node test intensity but its revenue is tied to cyclical chip capex and unit volumes, leaving it exposed to downturns despite a strong niche position.

Ichor Holdings.role: Ichor supplies gas and chemical fluid delivery subsystems to wafer-fab equipment OEMs; it sits in AI-infra as a deep cyclical supplier whose demand tracks semiconductor capex and carries thin margins and limited pricing power.

Advanced Energy Industries.role: Supplies precision power conversion and control systems for semiconductor manufacturing equipment, data centers, and industrial/medical markets; sits in AI-infra with moderate moat as a key enabler of fab tooling and data-center power, but earnings are exposed to semiconductor capex cycles.

Impinj.role: Impinj is the leading provider of RAIN RFID tag chips and reader systems for item-level connectivity in retail, logistics, and supply chains; it sits in AI-infra with moderate moat but cyclical, inventory-sensitive demand that recently turned negative YoY.

SiTime.role: SiTime makes MEMS-based precision timing chips replacing legacy quartz oscillators, with growing exposure to data-center/AI networking; it sits in AI-infra but as a smaller, more cyclical component supplier with meaningful but not dominant share.

Semtech.role: Mixed-signal and IoT connectivity chipmaker (LoRa, optical interconnect, edge wireless) with modest single-digit revenue growth; cyclical end-markets and moderate moat keep it mid-quality with elevated beta, while its data-center optical/CopperEdge exposure provides a partial AI-infra tilt.

Rambus.role: Rambus licenses memory interface IP and sells DDR5/PCIe chips benefiting from data-center memory bandwidth demand; modest reported YoY growth tempers the AI-tailwind narrative, placing it as a mid-quality cyclical-leaning infra play with a defensible but non-monopolistic IP moat.

Indie Semiconductor.role: Fabless designer of automotive mixed-signal and edge-AI chips for ADAS, radar, and connectivity; sits in disruptive given small revenue base, persistent unprofitability, flat recent YoY growth, and exposure to cyclical auto demand despite a credible long-run content-growth thesis.

ServiceNow.role: ServiceNow runs a sticky, subscription-based enterprise workflow platform now embedding GenAI (Now Assist) across IT/HR/customer service; high recurring revenue and deep enterprise lock-in make demand durable, placing it as a defensible AI-platform name with moderate permanence risk.

CrowdStrike.role: Cloud-native cybersecurity platform using AI/ML for endpoint and threat detection on recurring subscriptions; sits in AI-platform with durable recurring revenue and a strong moat, though premium valuation and the lingering July 2024 outage temper certainty. Note: the fresh ~5.9% YoY appears understa

Snowflake.role: Cloud-native data warehousing and analytics platform monetizing AI/ML workloads on consumption pricing; durable recurring usage but faces hyperscaler competition and rich valuation, placing it as a quality but premium-priced AI-platform name.

Datadog.role: Datadog is a cloud observability and monitoring platform expanding into AI/LLM monitoring; recurring usage-based SaaS revenue gives durable demand, but elevated valuation and competitive monitoring market temper its moat and permanence.

MongoDB.role: MongoDB sells a developer-focused NoSQL document database (Atlas cloud) positioned as a backend for AI-native apps; recurring consumption revenue gives moderate durability, but decelerating ~6% TTM growth, modest moat versus hyperscaler databases, and a premium multiple place it as a mid-quality AI-

ServiceTitan.role: Cloud and AI-enabled operating software for residential/commercial trades (HVAC, plumbing, electrical); durable recurring revenue tied to non-discretionary services, but still unprofitable on a GAAP basis with a premium valuation and concentrated end-market exposure.

Confluent.role: Confluent commercializes managed Apache Kafka for real-time data streaming that increasingly feeds AI pipelines; recurring consumption revenue and a credible streaming franchise place it in AI-platform, but decelerating ~5% reported YoY growth, ongoing GAAP losses, and competition from cloud-native

Elastic.role: Elastic provides search, observability, and security analytics built on its Elasticsearch engine, now extending into vector/RAG search for AI workloads; recurring subscription revenue and sticky enterprise deployments support durability, but modest reported YoY growth and competition from cloud-nati

GitLab.role: GitLab provides a unified DevSecOps platform with embedded AI (Duo) for code generation and workflow automation; recurring subscription revenue and enterprise stickiness offset its decelerating ~6% reported YoY and competition from GitHub Copilot, placing it as a credible but non-dominant AI-platfor

HubSpot.role: HubSpot is a mid-market CRM/marketing SaaS vendor embedding AI features (Breeze) into recurring subscriptions; durable recurring revenue and entrenched SMB base place it as a quality AI-platform name, but decelerating ~5% reported YoY growth and crowded competition from Salesforce/Microsoft cap its

Samsara.role: Samsara provides a cloud IoT platform connecting physical-operations fleets and equipment with AI-driven analytics on recurring subscription revenue; durable retention and large TAM offset by rich valuation and competition keep it mid-quality disruptive-platform.

Tempus AI.role: Tempus provides AI-driven precision medicine via genomic sequencing and a clinical data platform, but persistent unprofitability, modest reported YoY growth, and a competitive diagnostics landscape place it in the speculative disruptive bucket.

C3.ai.role: C3.ai sells enterprise AI application software but remains unprofitable with modest revenue, customer concentration, and uncertain moat, making it a speculative single name with elevated terminal-loss risk.

SoundHound AI.role: Small-cap pure-play in voice/conversational AI for autos, restaurants, and IoT; unprofitable with modest revenue base and competitive pressure from hyperscalers, placing it firmly in speculative disruptive territory.

Pure Storage.role: All-flash enterprise storage vendor benefiting from AI data-pipeline and hyperscaler design wins; solid but competes against larger storage incumbents, sitting as a quality mid-cap rather than a moated leader.

Dell Technologies.role: Dell builds AI-optimized servers and infrastructure for enterprises and hyperscalers; strong backlog and ISG momentum anchor demand, but low-margin, capex-cyclical hardware and thin pricing power limit earnings durability and moat.

Super Micro Computer.role: Assembles GPU-dense AI server and rack systems with strong Nvidia ties, but thin margins, accounting/governance overhangs, and a commoditized integrator model leave it exposed to capex cycles and competitive pricing pressure.

Hewlett Packard Enterprise.role: Legacy enterprise hardware vendor with growing AI server and Juniper-bolstered networking exposure, but thin margins and cyclical capex dependence cap quality and moat.

Arista Networks.role: Arista supplies high-performance Ethernet switching for hyperscale and AI data-center back-end networks; strong moat in cloud-tier switching but revenue is tied to hyperscaler capex cycles, hence moderate capex elasticity.

Ciena.role: Ciena supplies optical networking and coherent transport gear connecting data centers and telecom backbones; it benefits from AI-driven bandwidth growth but sells into lumpy carrier/hyperscaler capex cycles, anchoring it mid-tier in quality and cyclicality.

Fabrinet.role: Specialized contract manufacturer of optical/photonic components for datacom and telecom, riding AI-driven transceiver demand; durable customer relationships but cyclical exposure to hyperscaler capex and concentrated key customers temper its moat and earnings stability.

Western Digital.role: Western Digital makes hard-disk and flash storage benefiting from AI-driven nearline data center demand, but its cyclical, commoditized memory exposure and capex-sensitive end markets keep earnings durability and pricing power moderate.

Seagate Technology.role: Leading maker of high-capacity hard disk drives benefiting from AI-driven data-center storage demand, but earnings are cyclical and exposed to NAND substitution and capex swings.

Iron Mountain.role: Storage/records REIT pivoting to data-center colocation; small but fast-growing DC segment rides AI demand while legacy storage cash flows anchor durability, hence AI-infra with moderate permanence.

Constellation Energy.role: Largest US operator of nuclear generation supplying baseload clean power increasingly contracted to hyperscaler datacenters; sits in defensive given regulated/recurring power demand, but AI-driven upside is a smaller incremental piece of a mature utility-scale revenue base.

Vistra.role: Independent power producer with nuclear and gas generation positioned to supply rising data-center electricity demand; durable recurring power sales but cyclical commodity exposure and fragmented merchant-power competition cap its moat.

Talen Energy.role: Independent power producer with nuclear (Susquehanna) and gas assets supplying data-center load via co-location deals; sits in AI-infra as a picks-and-shovels power enabler, with recurring electricity demand giving resilience but exposure to power-price cycles and regulatory risk on behind-the-meter

NuScale Power.role: Pre-commercial SMR developer with NRC-certified design but negligible revenue and no operating reactors yet; speculative, capex-heavy, and reliant on long-dated utility/government commitments, placing it firmly in early-stage disruptive territory.

Eaton.role: Diversified electrical equipment maker supplying power distribution, switchgear, and backup systems to data centers; rides AI buildout demand but remains a cyclical industrial with broad non-AI exposure.

Quanta Services.role: Quanta is a leading electrical/grid infrastructure contractor benefiting from data-center power buildout and grid modernization, but as a project-based services firm its backlog is exposed to utility/capex cycles, placing it as a quality-cyclical AI-infra enabler rather than a pure secular play.

nVent Electric.role: nVent supplies electrical connection, protection, and liquid-cooling enclosure solutions, with a growing data-center power and thermal management segment benefiting from AI buildouts; it sits as a quality mid-cap AI-infra pick-and-shovel name whose demand is partly tied to discretionary data-center

Nutanix.role: Subscription-based hyperconverged infrastructure and hybrid cloud software vendor with recurring ARR; sits as a quality mid-cap platform with durable but competitively pressured demand against VMware and hyperscaler-native options.

Cloudflare.role: Cloudflare runs a global edge network offering security, CDN, and serverless compute, now positioning Workers AI for distributed inference; durable recurring SaaS revenue and a credible edge moat justify mid-tier quality, but a rich valuation and modest current AI monetization temper the durability

Twilio.role: Twilio provides cloud communications APIs (messaging, voice) and is layering AI-driven customer engagement onto its CDP and Segment data assets; mid-growth, usage-based recurring revenue gives moderate durability but it faces fragmented competition and modest ~4% organic growth, capping the AI premi

Dynatrace.role: Dynatrace provides AI-driven unified observability and security for cloud environments with high recurring SaaS revenue; it sits as a quality mid-cap platform with durable subscription economics but faces strong competition from Datadog, Splunk, and incumbents, limiting moat dominance.

Fortinet.role: Fortinet is a profitable network security vendor with strong firewall/SASE franchise and recurring subscription revenue; mature growth and durable cash flows place it as a defensive AI-security beneficiary rather than a hyper-growth disruptor.

Palo Alto Networks.role: Leading enterprise cybersecurity vendor consolidating network, cloud, and SOC security with AI-enhanced platformization; durable recurring revenue and non-discretionary spend place it as a quality AI-platform name, though decelerating ~7% reported growth and a rich multiple temper upside.

Zscaler.role: Cloud-native zero-trust security platform with recurring subscription revenue; durable demand and a strong SASE position, but decelerating growth and high multiple keep it mid-quality rather than dominant.

SentinelOne.role: AI-native endpoint and XDR security vendor whose recurring SaaS revenue is fairly resilient, but it sits in a competitive field behind CrowdStrike/Microsoft with modest share and not-yet-durable GAAP profitability, hence mid quality and elevated beta.

Veeva Systems.role: Vertical SaaS provider of cloud and data software for pharma/biotech R&D and commercial operations; deeply embedded, recurring-revenue franchise with strong margins makes it a defensive name where AI is an incremental feature rather than a transformative growth driver, hence modest growth and high d

Workday.role: Cloud HCM and financial management SaaS embedding AI/ML into enterprise back-office workflows; durable recurring subscription revenue with high switching costs places it as a quality platform, though slowing growth and crowded competition cap upside.

Atlassian.role: Atlassian provides team collaboration and developer tooling (Jira, Confluence) with embedded AI (Rovo); recurring subscription revenue and strong workflow lock-in give durability, but mid-teens growth and rich valuation keep it a quality mid-cap rather than a dominant secular winner.

DoubleVerify.role: DoubleVerify provides digital ad measurement, fraud detection and brand-safety verification; decelerating ~2% YoY revenue and a duopolistic but contestable market (vs. IAS) place it as a mid-quality growth name exposed to discretionary ad-budget cyclicality.

monday.com.role: Cloud-based work OS for project/workflow management adding AI features; recurring SaaS revenue and sticky seats give decent durability, but it competes in a fragmented collaboration market without a dominant moat.

Klaviyo.role: Klaviyo is a SaaS marketing automation and CRM platform for e-commerce that layers AI-driven segmentation and personalization on owned customer data; recurring subscription revenue and SMB/mid-market stickiness give it moderate durability, but it competes in a fragmented martech market and remains e

Braze.role: Braze provides a SaaS customer-engagement and messaging platform layering AI personalization atop recurring subscription revenue; solid retention but in a competitive marketing-software niche with thin profitability, placing it in mid-quality disruptive-leaning platform territory.

Rubrik.role: Rubrik provides cloud-based data security, backup, and cyber-recovery software with growing AI-driven threat detection; recurring subscription revenue and non-discretionary security spend support resilience, but it remains unprofitable in a competitive market against established backup/security vend

CyberArk.role: CyberArk is the leading privileged access management vendor expanding into machine/AI agent identity security; recurring subscription revenue and non-discretionary security spend make it durable, though its premium valuation and modest scale relative to the broad identity TAM temper the score.

Gitlab.role: GitLab provides a single end-to-end DevSecOps platform with AI features (Duo) layered on subscription software; recurring revenue and sticky developer workflows support durability, but slowing growth, a fragmented competitive field against GitHub/Microsoft, and a premium valuation keep dominance mod

Marqeta.role: Marqeta provides API-driven card-issuing and payment-processing infrastructure for fintechs; the 'AI payments' framing is thin, so it sits as a customer-concentrated, modest-growth disruptive fintech with weak moat and cyclical transaction-volume exposure.

Penguin Solutions.role: Penguin Solutions builds and integrates AI/HPC computing clusters and memory solutions; a smaller systems integrator with modest moat and cyclical capex exposure, sitting in AI-infra with mid-tier durability and elevated single-name volatility.

Pegatron via Sanmina.role: Contract electronics manufacturer (EMS) building AI server and infrastructure hardware for OEM customers; sits in AI-infra but with thin margins, low pricing power, and high exposure to cyclical capex swings, making earnings durability and moat modest.

Jabil.role: Contract manufacturer (EMS) producing AI server racks, networking, and cloud infrastructure hardware; sits in AI-infra but with thin margins, customer concentration, and capex-cyclical demand that caps moat and earnings durability.

Flex.role: Flex is a diversified EMS/contract manufacturer whose data-center and cloud power/rack-integration segment benefits from AI buildouts, but most revenue is cyclical, low-margin, and customer-dependent, limiting moat and pricing power.

Celestica.role: Contract manufacturer and hardware platform solutions provider whose CCS segment builds AI/ML compute and networking gear for hyperscalers; sits in AI-infra but carries cyclical EMS margins, concentrated customer risk, and exposure to hyperscaler capex swings.

Amphenol.role: Diversified interconnect and sensor maker whose high-speed cable and connector products ride AI data-center buildouts; broad end-market base softens cyclicality but the AI-specific slice remains capex-linked, placing it as durable mid-quality AI-infra.

Corning.role: Diversified materials maker whose optical communications segment supplies fiber and connectivity for AI data-center buildouts; sits in AI-infra with moderate growth and solid moat, but the AI-relevant slice is part of a larger cyclical, capex-sensitive business.

Vertiv peer nLight.role: nLight makes high-power semiconductor and fiber lasers for industrial, microfabrication, and defense/aerospace markets; it sits in AI-infra as a hardware supplier but remains a small, cyclical, often-unprofitable name with heavy defense exposure and modest growth, hence elevated risk scores.

Keysight Technologies.role: Keysight provides electronic test and measurement instruments increasingly used to validate AI networking, high-speed interconnects, and datacenter silicon; it sits in AI-infra with moderate moat and cyclical exposure since instrument demand tracks customer R&D and capex budgets.

Bloom Energy.role: Bloom makes solid-oxide fuel cells increasingly positioned as on-site primary power for data centers facing grid constraints; demand is real but capex-driven and competitive, with thin/inconsistent profitability keeping it mid-quality and cyclical.

Comfort Systems USA.role: Mechanical/electrical contractor installing HVAC and cooling systems, with a fast-growing data-center segment riding AI capex; sits in AI-infra but exposed to construction cyclicality if capex freezes.

Sterling Infrastructure.role: Provides site development and foundation/earthwork services for large data-center builds via its E-Infrastructure segment; benefits from the hyperscaler buildout but earnings are project-cyclical and tied to discretionary capex, with a fragmented competitive field limiting moat.

Dycom Industries.role: Specialty contractor building fiber/telecom and increasingly hyperscaler data-center network infrastructure; sits in AI-infra as a picks-and-shovels beneficiary but with cyclical, capex-dependent project revenue and customer concentration that caps moat and durability.

Vertiv peer Modine.role: Diversified thermal-management maker whose data-center cooling segment rides AI buildout, but legacy auto/HVAC exposure and cyclical capex sensitivity temper durability and moat.

NextEra Energy.role: Largest U.S. regulated utility and renewables developer positioned to supply growing data-center power demand; sits as a low-beta defensive name with durable regulated cash flows but only indirect, capacity-constrained AI upside.

Cameco.role: Cameco is one of the largest uranium miners and nuclear fuel-cycle providers; it benefits indirectly from AI-driven power demand reviving nuclear, but its earnings are commodity-cyclical and contract-paced, sitting as a quality-mid infrastructure play with moderate moat and durable but price-sensiti

Doximity.role: Doximity runs the dominant US physician professional network monetized via pharma/health-system marketing and adding AI clinical workflow tools; durable engaged user base and high margins justify a quality mid-cap profile, but it sits in AI-platform rather than infra given software economics and ad-

Silicon Laboratories.role: Fabless supplier of wireless connectivity SoCs (Bluetooth, Zigbee, Wi-Fi, Thread) for IoT edge devices; a credible niche player in a growing but competitive and cyclical market, recovering from an inventory-driven downturn, hence mid-tier quality and elevated valuation on depressed near-term earning

Ambarella.role: Fabless designer of low-power edge AI vision SoCs for automotive, security cameras and robotics; sits as a small, competitive, still barely-profitable niche player whose growth depends on cyclical camera/auto design wins, hence moderate quality and elevated risk.

Kulicke and Soffa.role: KLIC makes wire bonding and advanced packaging assembly equipment for semiconductor back-end; it benefits from advanced-packaging tailwinds tied to AI but sits in cyclical capex-driven equipment demand with modest growth and moderate moat, hence AI-infra with elevated cyclicality.

CEVA.role: CEVA licenses DSP and edge-AI IP embedded in wireless, sensor and IoT chips, earning royalties and license fees; small revenue base, modest growth and a real but crowded IP niche keep it a volatile mid-quality edge play rather than a durable leader.

SkyWater Technology.role: US-based specialty semiconductor foundry serving defense, aerospace and advanced niche markets; thin margins, sub-scale versus leading foundries, and reliance on lumpy program-based demand place it as a volatile, semi-speculative infrastructure play with moderate strategic (domestic-sourcing) suppor

Vertiv peer / Modine skip.role: Provider of data-center cooling and power infrastructure levered to AI buildouts; sits in AI-infra with moderate moat but meaningful cyclicality tied to hyperscaler capex cycles.

Cipher Mining.role: Bitcoin miner pivoting data-center capacity toward AI/HPC hosting; revenue is small, crypto-cyclical, and the AI compute pivot is early and unproven, so it sits as a speculative, high-beta name with elevated terminal-drawdown risk.

IREN Limited.role: Bitcoin miner pivoting to AI/HPC cloud and GPU colocation using owned data-center capacity; sits as speculative AI-infra because its AI revenue is nascent, capital-intensive, and exposed to both crypto cyclicality and GPU-demand timing.

Core Scientific.role: Former bitcoin miner pivoting to AI/HPC colocation hosting (anchored by a large CoreWeave contract); sits in AI-infra but with high beta and moderate permanence risk given customer concentration, thin margins, and a capital-intensive, still-transitioning business model.

Applied Digital.role: Applied Digital builds and leases GPU data center capacity for AI workloads; it sits in speculative AI-infra given thin margins, heavy capex reliance, customer concentration, and an unproven path to durable profitability despite strong tenant demand.

TeraWulf.role: Former bitcoin miner pivoting to AI/HPC data-center hosting, offering power-rich sites to compute tenants; sits in disruptive with high beta and low moat given nascent contracts, capital-intensity, and dependence on a few anchor customers.

Nebius Group.role: Nebius is a GPU-focused AI cloud provider (post-Yandex spinoff) renting Nvidia compute to AI developers; strong revenue growth but pre-profit, capex-heavy, and competing against hyperscalers/neoclouds keeps its moat thin and demand tied to the AI capex cycle.

Vertiv peer / Comfort skip.role: A data-center power and cooling infrastructure supplier riding AI buildout demand; placed in AI-infra with moderate moat and meaningful capex-cyclicality since its revenue depends on discretionary hyperscaler capex that can pause in a credit freeze.

Centrus Energy.role: Centrus enriches uranium including sole US-licensed HALEU production for advanced reactors; strategically positioned but small-revenue, policy-dependent, and cyclical, hence disruptive with moderate permanence risk.

GE Vernova peer / Emcor.role: EMCOR is a mechanical/electrical construction and facilities services contractor riding data-center and electrification demand; its AI exposure is real but project-based and cyclical, making earnings sensitive to a capex freeze despite a large served market.

Powell Industries.role: Powell makes switchgear, bus systems, and integrated electrical distribution for industrial, utility, oil/gas, and increasingly data-center customers; it benefits indirectly from AI-driven power buildout but remains a cyclical project-based hardware supplier with modest recent growth and a fragmente

Vertiv peer / AAON.role: AAON designs HVAC and thermal management equipment with a growing data center liquid/air cooling segment; it sits mid-tier as a diversified building-systems maker whose AI-data-center exposure is real but a minority of revenue, leaving it partly cyclical on construction and capex.

GDS Holdings.role: Leading Chinese carrier-neutral data center operator serving domestic hyperscalers and enterprises; solid recurring colocation revenue but burdened by high leverage, China regulatory/macro risk, and heavy capex intensity, placing it as a cyclical AI-infra name.

Grid Dynamics.role: Digital engineering and AI consulting firm serving enterprise clients; sits in disruptive due to modest scale, fragmented competition against large IT-services incumbents, and project-based revenue that is discretionary and cyclical during capex freezes.

Innodata.role: Innodata provides data engineering and annotation services for training AI/LLM models, with revenue concentrated among a few Big Tech customers; strong secular tailwind but customer concentration, project-based revenue, and a fragmented, competitive labeling market keep durability and moat modest.

Freshworks.role: Mid-market CRM/ITSM SaaS vendor layering AI copilots onto its suite; recurring subscription revenue provides resilience, but slowing ~4% YoY growth, a crowded competitive field against Salesforce/ServiceNow/Zoho, and modest moat keep quality and dominance moderate.

Nice Ltd.role: Leading provider of cloud contact-center (CCaaS) and workforce-optimization software increasingly infused with generative AI; recurring SaaS revenue and strong CX market position give durable earnings, but growth has decelerated amid competition and macro softness, placing it as a quality mid-cap AI

Docebo peer / DigitalOcean.role: DigitalOcean is a developer/SMB-focused cloud provider adding GPU capacity (via Paperspace) for AI workloads, but its modest ~6% growth, small scale, and intense competition from hyperscalers cap its AI upside and moat.
